summary.text: As it turns out, all the great inspiring leaders and organizations in the world, whether it's Apple or Martin Luther King or the Wright brothers, they all think, act and communicate the exact same way. In other words, when we communicate from the outside in, yes, people can understand vast amounts of complicated information like features and benefits and facts and figures. When we can communicate from the inside out, we're talking directly to the part of the brain that controls behavior, and then we allow people to rationalize it with the tangible things we say and do. He held a seat at Harvard and worked at the Smithsonian and was extremely well-connected; he knew all the big minds of the day. From the time TiVo came out about eight or nine years ago to this current day, they are the single highest-quality product on the market, hands down, there is no dispute.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
How do you explain
when things don't go as we assume?

segments.label: positive
segments.text: Or better, how do you explain

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
when others are able to achieve things
that seem to defy all of the assumptions?

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: For example:

segments.label: positive
segments.text: Why is Apple so innovative?

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Year after year, after year,

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
they're more innovative
than all their competition.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: And yet, they're just a computer company.

segments.label: positive
segments.text: They're just like everyone else.

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
They have the same access
to the same talent,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: the same agencies,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: the same consultants, the same media.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
Then why is it that they seem
to have something different?

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
Why is it that Martin Luther King
led the Civil Rights Movement?

segments.label: negative
segments.text:
He wasn't the only man
who suffered in pre-civil rights America,

segments.label: negative
segments.text:
and he certainly wasn't
the only great orator of the day.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Why him?

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: And why is it that the Wright brothers

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
were able to figure out controlled,
powered man flight

segments.label: positive
segments.text: when there were certainly other teams

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
who were better qualified,
better funded --

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
and they didn't achieve
powered man flight,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: and the Wright brothers beat them to it.

segments.label: positive
segments.text: There's something else at play here.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
About three and a half years ago,
I made a discovery.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
And this discovery profoundly changed
my view on how I thought the world worked,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
and it even profoundly changed the way
in which I operate in it.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: As it turns out, there's a pattern.

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
As it turns out, all the great inspiring
leaders and organizations in the world,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
whether it's Apple or Martin Luther King
or the Wright brothers,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
they all think, act and communicate
the exact same way.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
And it's the complete opposite
to everyone else.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: All I did was codify it,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
and it's probably
the world's simplest idea.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: I call it the golden circle.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Why? How? What?

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: This little idea explains

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
why some organizations and some leaders
are able to inspire where others aren't.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Let me define the terms really quickly.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
Every single person, every single
organization on the planet

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: knows what they do, 100 percent.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Some know how they do it,

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
whether you call it
your differentiated value proposition

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: or your proprietary process or your USP.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
But very, very few people or organizations
know why they do what they do.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
And by "why" I don't mean
"to make a profit."

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: That's a result. It's always a result.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: By "why," I mean: What's your purpose?

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: What's your cause? What's your belief?

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Why does your organization exist?

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Why do you get out of bed in the morning?

segments.label: positive
segments.text: And why should anyone care?

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: As a result, the way we think, we act,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
the way we communicate
is from the outside in, it's obvious.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
We go from the clearest thing
to the fuzziest thing.

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
But the inspired leaders
and the inspired organizations --

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
regardless of their size,
regardless of their industry --

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
all think, act and communicate
from the inside out.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Let me give you an example.

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
I use Apple because they're easy
to understand and everybody gets it.

segments.label: positive
segments.text: If Apple were like everyone else,

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
a marketing message from them
might sound like this:

segments.label: positive
segments.text: "We make great computers.

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
They're beautifully designed,
simple to use and user friendly.

segments.label: positive
segments.text: Want to buy one?"

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: "Meh."

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: That's how most of us communicate.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
That's how most marketing
and sales are done,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: that's how we communicate interpersonally.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: We say what we do,

segments.label: positive
segments.text: we say how we're different or better

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: and we expect some sort of a behavior,

segments.label: positive
segments.text: a purchase, a vote, something like that.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Here's our new law firm:

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
We have the best lawyers
with the biggest clients,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: we always perform for our clients.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Here's our new car:

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
It gets great gas mileage,
it has leather seats.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Buy our car.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: But it's uninspiring.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Here's how Apple actually communicates.

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
"Everything we do,
we believe in challenging the status quo.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: We believe in thinking differently.

segments.label: positive
segments.text: The way we challenge the status quo

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
is by making our products
beautifully designed,

segments.label: positive
segments.text: simple to use and user friendly.

segments.label: positive
segments.text: We just happen to make great computers.

segments.label: positive
segments.text: Want to buy one?"

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Totally different, right?

segments.label: positive
segments.text: You're ready to buy a computer from me.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
I just reversed
the order of the information.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
What it proves to us is
that people don't buy what you do;

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: people buy why you do it.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
This explains why
every single person in this room

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
is perfectly comfortable buying
a computer from Apple.

segments.label: positive
segments.text: But we're also perfectly comfortable

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
buying an MP3 player from Apple,
or a phone from Apple,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: or a DVR from Apple.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
As I said before,
Apple's just a computer company.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
Nothing distinguishes them structurally
from any of their competitors.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
Their competitors are equally qualified
to make all of these products.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: In fact, they tried.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
A few years ago, Gateway
came out with flat-screen TVs.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
They're eminently qualified
to make flat-screen TVs.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
They've been making
flat-screen monitors for years.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Nobody bought one.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Dell came out with MP3 players and PDAs,

segments.label: positive
segments.text: and they make great quality products,

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
and they can make perfectly
well-designed products --

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: and nobody bought one.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
In fact, talking about it now,
we can't even imagine

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: buying an MP3 player from Dell.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
Why would you buy one
from a computer company?

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: But we do it every day.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
People don't buy what you do;
they buy why you do it.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
The goal is not to do business
with everybody who needs what you have.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
The goal is to do business with people
who believe what you believe.

segments.label: positive
segments.text: Here's the best part:

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
None of what I'm telling you
is my opinion.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
It's all grounded
in the tenets of biology.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Not psychology, biology.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
If you look at a cross-section
of the human brain,

segments.label: negative
segments.text:
from the top down,
the human brain is actually broken

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: into three major components

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
that correlate perfectly
with the golden circle.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Our newest brain, our Homo sapien brain,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: our neocortex,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: corresponds with the "what" level.

segments.label: positive
segments.text: The neocortex is responsible

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
for all of our rational
and analytical thought and language.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
The middle two sections make up
our limbic brains,

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
and our limbic brains are responsible
for all of our feelings,

segments.label: positive
segments.text: like trust and loyalty.

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
It's also responsible
for all human behavior,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: all decision-making,

segments.label: negative
segments.text: and it has no capacity for language.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
In other words, when we communicate
from the outside in,

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
yes, people can understand vast
amounts of complicated information

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
like features and benefits
and facts and figures.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: It just doesn't drive behavior.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
When we can communicate
from the inside out,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
we're talking directly
to the part of the brain

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: that controls behavior,

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
and then we allow people to rationalize it
with the tangible things we say and do.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: This is where gut decisions come from.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
Sometimes you can give somebody
all the facts and figures,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
and they say, "I know
what all the facts and details say,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: but it just doesn't feel right."

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
Why would we use that verb,
it doesn't "feel" right?

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
Because the part of the brain
that controls decision-making

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: doesn't control language.

segments.label: positive
segments.text: The best we can muster up is,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
"I don't know. 
It just doesn't feel right."

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
Or sometimes you say you're leading
with your heart or soul.

segments.label: negative
segments.text:
I hate to break it to you,
those aren't other body parts

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: controlling your behavior.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
It's all happening here
in your limbic brain,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
the part of the brain that controls
decision-making and not language.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
But if you don't know
why you do what you do,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
and people respond
to why you do what you do,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: then how will you ever get people

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
to vote for you,
or buy something from you,

segments.label: positive
segments.text: or, more importantly, be loyal

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
and want to be a part
of what it is that you do.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
The goal is not just to sell
to people who need what you have;

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
the goal is to sell to people
who believe what you believe.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
The goal is not just
to hire people who need a job;

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
it's to hire people
who believe what you believe.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: I always say that, you know,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
if you hire people just because they can
do a job, they'll work for your money,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: but if they believe what you believe,

segments.label: negative
segments.text:
they'll work for you with blood
and sweat and tears.

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
Nowhere else is there a better example
than with the Wright brothers.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
Most people don't know
about Samuel Pierpont Langley.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: And back in the early 20th century,

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
the pursuit of powered man flight
was like the dot com of the day.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Everybody was trying it.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
And Samuel Pierpont Langley
had, what we assume,

segments.label: positive
segments.text: to be the recipe for success.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Even now, you ask people,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
"Why did your product
or why did your company fail?"

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
and people always give
you the same permutation

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: of the same three things:

segments.label: negative
segments.text:
under-capitalized, the wrong people,
bad market conditions.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
It's always the same three things,
so let's explore that.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Samuel Pierpont Langley

segments.label: negative
segments.text:
was given 50,000 dollars
by the War Department

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: to figure out this flying machine.

segments.label: negative
segments.text: Money was no problem.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: He held a seat at Harvard

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
and worked at the Smithsonian
and was extremely well-connected;

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: he knew all the big minds of the day.

segments.label: positive
segments.text: He hired the best minds money could find

segments.label: positive
segments.text: and the market conditions were fantastic.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
The New York Times
followed him around everywhere,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: and everyone was rooting for Langley.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
Then how come we've never heard
of Samuel Pierpont Langley?

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: A few hundred miles away in Dayton, Ohio,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Orville and Wilbur Wright,

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
they had none of what we consider
to be the recipe for success.

segments.label: negative
segments.text: They had no money;

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
they paid for their dream
with the proceeds from their bicycle shop.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
Not a single person
on the Wright brothers' team

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: had a college education,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: not even Orville or Wilbur.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
And The New York Times
followed them around nowhere.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: The difference was,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
Orville and Wilbur were driven by a cause,
by a purpose, by a belief.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
They believed that if they could
figure out this flying machine,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: it'll change the course of the world.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Samuel Pierpont Langley was different.

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
He wanted to be rich,
and he wanted to be famous.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: He was in pursuit of the result.

segments.label: positive
segments.text: He was in pursuit of the riches.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: And lo and behold, look what happened.

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
The people who believed
in the Wright brothers' dream

segments.label: negative
segments.text:
worked with them with blood
and sweat and tears.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: The others just worked for the paycheck.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
They tell stories of how every time
the Wright brothers went out,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
they would have to take
five sets of parts,

segments.label: negative
segments.text:
because that's how many times
they would crash before supper.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: And, eventually, on December 17th, 1903,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: the Wright brothers took flight,

segments.label: negative
segments.text:
and no one was there
to even experience it.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: We found out about it a few days later.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
And further proof that Langley
was motivated by the wrong thing:

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: the day the Wright brothers took flight,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: he quit.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: He could have said,

segments.label: positive
segments.text: "That's an amazing discovery, guys,

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
and I will improve
upon your technology," but he didn't.

segments.label: negative
segments.text:
He wasn't first, he didn't get rich,
he didn't get famous, so he quit.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
People don't buy what you do;
they buy why you do it.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: If you talk about what you believe,

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
you will attract those
who believe what you believe.

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
But why is it important to attract
those who believe what you believe?

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
Something called the law
of diffusion of innovation,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
if you don't know the law,
you know the terminology.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
The first 2.5% of our population
are our innovators.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
The next 13.5% of our population
are our early adopters.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: The next 34% are your early majority,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: your late majority and your laggards.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
The only reason these people
buy touch-tone phones

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
is because you can't buy
rotary phones anymore.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: (Laughter)

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
We all sit at various places
at various times on this scale,

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
but what the law of diffusion
of innovation tells us

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
is that if you want mass-market success
or mass-market acceptance of an idea,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
you cannot have it
until you achieve this tipping point

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
between 15 and 18 percent
market penetration,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: and then the system tips.

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
I love asking businesses,
"What's your conversion on new business?"

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
They love to tell you,
"It's about 10 percent," proudly.

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
Well, you can trip
over 10% of the customers.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: We all have about 10% who just "get it."

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: That's how we describe them, right?

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
That's like that gut feeling,
"Oh, they just get it."

segments.label: negative
segments.text:
The problem is: How do you
find the ones that get it

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
before doing business
versus the ones who don't get it?

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
So it's this here, this little gap
that you have to close,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
as Jeffrey Moore calls it,
"Crossing the Chasm" --

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
because, you see, the early majority
will not try something

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: until someone else has tried it first.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
And these guys, the innovators
and the early adopters,

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
they're comfortable
making those gut decisions.

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
They're more comfortable
making those intuitive decisions

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
that are driven by what
they believe about the world

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: and not just what product is available.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
These are the people who stood
in line for six hours

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: to buy an iPhone when they first came out,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
when you could have bought one
off the shelf the next week.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
These are the people
who spent 40,000 dollars

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
on flat-screen TVs
when they first came out,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
even though the technology
was substandard.

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
And, by the way, they didn't do it
because the technology was so great;

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: they did it for themselves.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: It's because they wanted to be first.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
People don't buy what you do;
they buy why you do it

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
and what you do simply proves
what you believe.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
In fact, people will do the things
that prove what they believe.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
The reason that person bought the iPhone
in the first six hours,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: stood in line for six hours,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
was because of what they believed
about the world,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: and how they wanted everybody to see them:

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: they were first.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
People don't buy what you do;
they buy why you do it.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: So let me give you a famous example,

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
a famous failure and a famous success
of the law of diffusion of innovation.

segments.label: negative
segments.text: First, the famous failure.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: It's a commercial example.

segments.label: positive
segments.text: As we said before, the recipe for success

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
is money and the right people
and the right market conditions.

segments.label: positive
segments.text: You should have success then.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Look at TiVo.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
From the time TiVo came out
about eight or nine years ago

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: to this current day,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
they are the single highest-quality
product on the market,

segments.label: negative
segments.text: hands down, there is no dispute.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: They were extremely well-funded.

segments.label: positive
segments.text: Market conditions were fantastic.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: I mean, we use TiVo as verb.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
I TiVo stuff on my piece-of-junk
Time Warner DVR all the time.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: (Laughter)

segments.label: negative
segments.text: But TiVo's a commercial failure.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: They've never made money.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: And when they went IPO,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: their stock was at about 30 or 40 dollars

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
and then plummeted,
and it's never traded above 10.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
In fact, I don't think
it's even traded above six,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: except for a couple of little spikes.

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
Because you see, when TiVo
launched their product,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: they told us all what they had.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
They said, "We have a product
that pauses live TV,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
skips commercials, rewinds live TV
and memorizes your viewing habits

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: without you even asking."

segments.label: negative
segments.text: And the cynical majority said,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: "We don't believe you.

segments.label: negative
segments.text: We don't need it. We don't like it.

segments.label: negative
segments.text: You're scaring us."

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: What if they had said,

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
"If you're the kind of person
who likes to have total control

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: over every aspect of your life,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: boy, do we have a product for you.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: It pauses live TV, skips commercials,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: memorizes your viewing habits, etc., etc."

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
People don't buy what you do;
they buy why you do it,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
and what you do simply serves
as the proof of what you believe.

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
Now let me give you a successful example
of the law of diffusion of innovation.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: In the summer of 1963,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
250,000 people showed up
on the mall in Washington

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: to hear Dr. King speak.

segments.label: negative
segments.text: They sent out no invitations,

segments.label: negative
segments.text:
and there was no website
to check the date.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: How do you do that?

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
Well, Dr. King
wasn't the only man in America

segments.label: positive
segments.text: who was a great orator.

segments.label: negative
segments.text:
He wasn't the only man
in America who suffered

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: in a pre-civil rights America.

segments.label: negative
segments.text: In fact, some of his ideas were bad.

segments.label: positive
segments.text: But he had a gift.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
He didn't go around telling people
what needed to change in America.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
He went around
and told people what he believed.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
"I believe, I believe, I believe,"
he told people.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: And people who believed what he believed

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
took his cause, and they made it
their own, and they told people.

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
And some of those people
created structures

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: to get the word out to even more people.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
And lo and behold,
250,000 people showed up

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
on the right day at the right time
to hear him speak.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: How many of them showed up for him?

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: Zero.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: They showed up for themselves.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: It's what they believed about America

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
that got them to travel
in a bus for eight hours

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
to stand in the sun in Washington
in the middle of August.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
It's what they believed,
and it wasn't about black versus white:

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: 25% of the audience was white.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
Dr. King believed that there are
two types of laws in this world:

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
those that are made by a higher authority
and those that are made by men.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
And not until all the laws
that are made by men

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
are consistent with the laws
made by the higher authority

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: will we live in a just world.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
It just so happened
that the Civil Rights Movement

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
was the perfect thing to help him
bring his cause to life.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
We followed, not for him,
but for ourselves.

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
By the way, he gave
the "I have a dream" speech,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: not the "I have a plan" speech.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: (Laughter)

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
Listen to politicians now,
with their comprehensive 12-point plans.

segments.label: negative
segments.text: They're not inspiring anybody.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
Because there are leaders
and there are those who lead.

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
Leaders hold a position
of power or authority,

segments.label: positive
segments.text: but those who lead inspire us.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
Whether they're individuals
or organizations,

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
we follow those who lead,
not because we have to,

segments.label: positive
segments.text: but because we want to.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text:
We follow those who lead, not for them,
but for ourselves.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: And it's those who start with "why"

segments.label: positive
segments.text:
that have the ability
to inspire those around them

segments.label: positive
segments.text: or find others who inspire them.

segments.label: positive
segments.text: Thank you very much.

segments.label: neutral
segments.text: (Applause)
